sentences.text: Irene, I-R-E-N-E, Boris, B-O-R-I-S.
C-- Chaja Rozenfeld, C-H-A-J-A R-O-Z-E-N-F-E-L-D.
No, really.
Yes, Chaja Gitl Jachnes.
That's my mother and my grandmother.
Chaja, C-H-A-J-A, Gitl, G-I-T-L, Gitl, Jachnes, J-A-C-H-N-E-S.
My age is 72.
Kostopol, K-O-S-T-O-P-O-L, and this is Poland, was Poland.
It was memories who they didn't want me to remember, because I was the sixth child born.
And my father passed away soon after.
He was sick from the first world war, because he was hidden in-- for the Russian, I guess, what it was this-- the First World War.
And he developed tuberculosis.
Symcha, S-Y-M-C-H-A, and my mother's was Gitl, G-I-T-L.
Rozenfeld.
Yes.
The one I remember was Abraham.
He was the fourth child.
And then was Szlema, [?
Szloma ?].
And I was the sixth one.
One sister, Babl-- B-A-B-L.
That's Babl.
That's what they used to call her.
The other one, I don't.
You were born in Kostopol.
Yes.
Kostopol was a medium-sized city, which in the 1930s, in the late '30s, became a bigger city because of the-- they found some stones there.
They used to.
And it came a large Polish population there to work.
And it became a prosperous city.
We had a railroad with a station.
We had a hotel.
We had three-- three synagogues and a shopping area, and a big outside market, which was on a Tuesday.
Yes.
Yes.
It was part of Poland till 1939.
Well, my father, I don't remember.
But my mother, we had some-- my mother-- we had people working for my mother.
And my mother used to sell foot-ware.
And were some Ukrainians, some Jewish.
And they worked, and my mother used to sell them.
She used to prepare all the buying and this.
And she was a good business lady.
I guess so.
I'm not so sure, because he was sick for a long time that I didn't have [? access ?] to talk to my father too much because he had tuberculosis for quite a few years.
And as I was the youngest and so, so they kept apart.
Yes.
It was-- it was from the house and-- what was the-- Aptaska 18.
That's what was the-- the address.
A-P-T-A-S-K-A, 18 in the Aptaska.
No, no, no, no.
Absolutely no.
Yes.
It was.
Yes, yes, in the old part.
No.
No, not ever.
My mother had about 10.
No.
Was, I guess, a few.
I don't remember exactly if I'll say, but a few Jewish and most, and a few Ukrainian.
My religious background was like 90% of the city were Orthodox.
Not ultra-Orthodox, but Orthodox, like observing Sabbath, holidays, kosher.
And that's it.
Observant, yes.
Just-- I remember just my grandmother on my father's side.
She lived in the same house we did.
Jachna.
J-A-C-H-N-A.
I was over-protected because my-- my mother had bad experience, whatever, with the first one.
And I was-- by the time, I was the only girl home.
And since my father passed away, my mother just put all here on me.
She was a very good mother, I have to say, good schooling.
She gave us the best of schooling she could in this time.
And we always had food on the table.
And beside this, my mother had in Boston, around Boston, she had four sisters.
And she had a mother here.
So they really send us a lot of money, just to keep it up, because my mother gave up the business.
She became very ill.
She gave out the business, so my aunts and my grandmother here supplied us with all the money.
[SIGHS] I was-- oh, it was probably about 12 till then, no more.
No more.
No more, no.
And then I became very ill.
I had like a kidneys infection in this time.
So, like I said, I was over-protected.
But my mother's family from Boston told her in a letter that don't worry about money.
Even they were poor people here.
But they always sending like $50 a month.
Everybody, you know, contribute, I think.
When When your mother's business closed, it was strictly because of her illness.
[INAUDIBLE] yes, strictly illness.
It didn't change too much.
No, it didn't change too much, no.
A matter of fact that my mother had help.
A cousin of my mother's came from a little village, and she stayed with us.
And she helped out with the kids.
Chaja too.
Your name was Chaja.
And the only thing my mother believed was a strong believer in education.
This I remember.
And my brother couldn't read so good.
Even in Europe, she had a tutor coming in every week.
And I'm-- for her, schooling was the most important thing in life.
Well, I went to public school.
It was no-name.
This time was one school for everybody.
And then my brothers, and then we went to a special teacher to learn Hebrew.
And Hebrew was start in the schools too.
No, no.
It was a mix.
Till 1938, was pretty good.
They had no differences.
We went to school.
We went to, like, was a Polish holiday, a national holiday.
We went to the synagogue.
And was the cantor there chanting, you know, bless the government and everything.
In 1939, start being a little bit older.
They said-- they start putting up some signs.
Don't buy from a Jew.
Buy from us.
And this was mostly in the Catholic section where the Polish church was.
And they say, everybody should go to his own.
And little things started.
And they knew-- they-- they tried to avoid this because nobody believed it, that this could happen, because we lived together, mostly.
Except it was a place like rest of the city that most Ukrainian people lived.
And they lived like for themselves.
But Polish and Jewish, and it was German that they mostly liked together.
No, no, I didn't.
It was-- you didn't have really till-- till the war and till '41, I think.
That's what they showed up.
No, it didn't start.
It-- when the Russian came in.
Oh, '39 before the Russians came in it was already-- you could feel the anti-Semitism.
You start feeling it.
Like I said, they start advertising don't buy from a Jew.
And really, you felt it when you went-- went out on the street.
You start feeling it.
Just plain, putting up signs.
Well, that's my-- my opinion, personal.
It was on the Catholic church, because they owned a lot of property.
And they owned stores.
And they had like a section, mostly Polish people lived there.
And-- and it was not Polacks, nobody else.
Because it-- I don't say it's the church.
It's near the church because they own property there.
They own stores.
And it started.
Yes, much, much, much.
Sure.
That-- when you didn't go out so much outside in the evening because they started throwing glasses.
And there, you know?
They somebody wore glasses, and so.
Little by little, you felt it.
And you-- you know, you were like isolated a little from the rest of population.
Almost, almost, yeah.
Not exactly, because I was out of school, and it started the summer.
And the air wasn't clear all that.
In Poland, there were seven grades.
But the curriculum was much tougher than here.
Seven grades, it was like high school here.
And it was the summer the war started.
Yes.
No, no, no.
[SIGHS] Well, almost nothing.
We used to go in the woods and pick up some mushrooms over there.
Well, and you know.
And you belonged to a Jewish organization, whatever it was there.
And friends, and that's all.
Saturday, you used to walk around the city.
It's like was the usual, you know, time in Poland.
Yeah we went to-- I was-- they call-- used to call Bital.
I guess it's B-I-T-A-L.
And it was a better-- I didn't know.
It was probably my friends and whatever there.
And then was a [?
Czomer ?] [?
Raziel ?] was [INAUDIBLE].
My brothers belonged to the [?
Czomer ?] [?
Raziel, ?] because most of the boys belonged there.
A Czomer, C-Z-O-M-E-R, [INAUDIBLE], R-A-A-Z-I-E-L.
That's I would spell it.
I don't know.
Yes, yes, 100%.
As far as I remember, no.
It was just the most social.
It was a social organization that-- it was a place to gather, that young people gathered.
And they had meetings and lectures and all the things.
We had theater, not as here, but we had.
We had a movie.
And most of the-- we had lectures from certain people who came in, most on the-- from the Zionist organizations.
They used to have lectures.
And they tried to persuade a lot of people going into Israel.
And a lot of them went.
And they had a kibbutz.
It's K-I-B-U-T-Z.
And a lot of young-- I would say 20 years old, or so, they went to work on the kibbutz.
And they live on hard work.
And from over there, if it was a occasion going to Israel, they all went.
So this was a big event that some 10 people or so went to Israel.
And they went to Israel to work on the-- you know, [INAUDIBLE], whatever, a kibbutzing.
They went to a kibbutz in Israel.
No, I don't, no.
No, no, I don't.
No, I really don't.
But I still have two girls who I met in Israel.
And they were the one they went to a kibbutz.
They're not alive anymore, but they went.
Yes, I did.
I don't know who record them.
But it was the-- the one who was, you know, from the organization.
He was chosen, and he arranged it.
It was not too much they could do.
It was not involvement.
The only thing they started, the Volksdeutsche.
They took him out and they send them to Germany.
[INAUDIBLE] of course, they wanted to go.
They went to Germany.
Volksdeutsche.
I guess that's German who were born in Poland.
That's the way I think of.
In-- starts being a little bit.
You could-- you know, you could smell it in the air.
Then came one, she was-- her name was [INAUDIBLE].
I mean, that's what I heard.
And she was in the Senate.
And she start looking for little troubles, like the slaughtering of cows shouldn't be the way it is, the ritual slaughtering.
And then a lot of things that she objected.
And this was the time when Hitler came to rise.
So she came to rise to her position this time.
It was somebody in the-- the Polish Senate, [INAUDIBLE].
I don't know either.
I mean, if I say, I know from remember just things we used to said.
Yes.
We could feel it that something's going to happen.
But if they-- the invasion came in, we went away to our little village and we stayed there about a week till things starts cooling down.
And then they said that's not the Germans, but the Russians going came in.
So then they came back to our house.
[POLISH]
I really don't know how you spell it.
[LAUGHS]
No, it wasn't in the newspapers so much.
It was the mouth-to-mouth.
People who-- I mean, they didn't know so much what went on, like the Kristallnacht, or so.
I didn't hear.
Maybe people who were older or more knowledgeable of those things, they probably hear.
But I didn't.
But you could smell the air wasn't clean anymore.
Yes.
It was a neighbor who was a [INAUDIBLE] guy.
And he wore glasses.
And he went out one night, and they broke his glasses.
But everybody thought it's somebody who's just, you know?
But nobody were thinking about what happened, that this is going to be.
No, absolutely no.
No.
Well, this time, the Russians were there in--
In September '41.
The were in our city.
They took-- this part of Poland went to the Russians.
And the other part were the-- the Germans.
So a lot of the people came to our city from the other part, from large-- from all the other cities they came to-- to the Russians.
99% Jews.
And life was rough, and it was really, if you didn't have connections, from food or whatever, everyone, like no more stores.
So every storekeeper tried to accumulate some shoes and this.
And during the period, you traded for food and other things.
And then when the Russians came in, it was bad, but not life-threatening.
We were still in the-- in the village.
And then they said that the Russian troops are coming.
Well, you couldn't go out.
They told you to stay in the-- in the houses.
The Russians.
And they brought a lot of-- nothing happened really too extreme, they should kill us or no.
Nothing happened.
And they tried to tell you that things are going to be fine and that the Germans wouldn't come anymore here.
But-- and this ended.
I mean, politics, the Russians didn't like to discuss politics.
It was part of their business.
Yes.
Yes, yes.
Oh, yeah, yeah.
Nothing.
They just went, not knowing where they go.
But they just left, you know, kept them here.
And they thought they most safe here, which a lot of them went back because no work, and no food, whatever they they took for them.
It was tough, very rough for them.
They used to come.
Nobody really occupied our house, no.
But they had-- it was a few hundred.
We had a big, big house, with apartments all around.
My mother, me, and two brothers and I.
My grandmother lived in the same house, but she had your own bedrooms and-- and kitchen.
And the other was rented to our tailor.
I don't remember even his name.
He had three rooms.
And there was another two rooms rented to somebody next door, which was a rich man, [?
Spivak. ?] And was rented to them because they lived next door.
They had their business.
And they rented from us two rooms.
My brothers were home.
Yes.
They lived-- my mother and three of us.
They were till-- till '41.
[INAUDIBLE].
That's-- is-- [INAUDIBLE] that's the 22nd of June the Germans invaded Russia.
It changed the things that food wasn't as it was before.
You had to stay in line for everything.
And it was just like temporary, everything.
I went to Russian school, which I learned a little bit.
You had to learn how to [? speak ?].
And then I wanted to go, and I went.
It was an Institute.
[SIGHS] I don't remember even, like in a laboratory, to be a-- to work in a laboratory.
But the summer, already, the Germans came in.
So-- but I went to learn the-- the language a little.
So it was mostly Ukrainian that they taught us, two things, Ukrainian and Russian.
I never learned the Ukrainian, but Russian, I learned.
Where were the troops garrisoned?
What do you mean, the troops?
They were-- they were on Kostopol, they had barracks there.
Would you know, I don't remember.
I know they used to take and they had like a public bath.
So they used to take them every day another-- used to march to the bathtubs, you know, to take a bath.
Oh, sure, they had.
They had.
They marched in the city.
And you were comfortable with the Russian army.
They made you comfortable.
No, no, absolutely not.
No, absolutely no.
They made you comfortable.
Yes, absolutely.
They used to trade, you know, buy some things to send home.
And there were a lot of Russian Jews who were in the army.
And they were in the-- the higher.
So like Saturday, Sunday, they used to come and look for Jewish girls.
And, I mean, they were very friendly, you know?
It was like, yeah.
And some of them--
No, no, I wasn't this time ready yet.
[LAUGHS]
This time, my mother accumulated a lot of leather goods.
And we traded.
How, I don't know.
I mean, I wasn't involved in those things.
Life wasn't easy.
Fine.
Whatever you want to do.
